text: I wanted to show you a video on how we perform robotic inguinal hernia repair on our patients. Hernias can occur in many spots but inguinal hernias are one of the most common surgeries that we perform. And these surgeries have really evolved in the last ten years. These surgeries used to only be done with large open incisions and we used to place big pieces of mesh in patients. Really in the past fifteen years we've developed robotic techniques that I really feel have helped my practice grow. These surgeries are now being done instead of opening patients with big incisions.

With small incisions and the reason I like that in my practice is our patients tend to have less pain, a little quicker recovery and as a surgeon using the robot I get to see a very high definition picture when I perform these surgeries. These surgeries usually involve three small incisions that we place in our patients. The first incision is placed around the belly button and three smaller incisions that are about eight millimeters in size are on either side of the belly button in the left and right side of the abdomen. Another benefit we have with these surgeries when we perform them robotically is that we can see both sides of the hernia. So for example, if a patient has a hernia on the left side, we can also look at the right side. So instead of making two cuts, we can do both sides of the patient with the same three incisions.

The video shows the hernia exposed in the center of the surgical field. The surgeon dissects surrounding anatomy to clearly visualize the hernia defect. A lightweight mesh is unrolled and placed over the defect to cover it completely.

This particular mesh is called a pro grip mesh and I like it because in the past we had to suture or tack these meshes in place. These new meshes in particular, this pro grip mesh has almost a velcro texture that sticks to the tissue. so we don't have to tack it in place and the issue with tacks and permanent sutures is many times we could have complications by catching nerves or having bleeding these new meshes have found to be very effective and actually lightweight and very unprone to infection so we've had very good results also with the robot

We can use it to suture and the robot really helps surgeons to suture, to see clearly. In fact, there's actually part of the software of the robot takes away small tremors from the hand and make things a little bit easier for the surgeon to manipulate tissue. This would be something that would be very hard to do laparoscopically or open in the traditional techniques.
The mesh is positioned over the hernia site, and the hernia sac has been removed. The surgeon is closing the hernia cover and freeing a piece of adhesions.
